1: A revelation of God: Psalm 75 is an Asaphite Psalm of communal thanksgiving framed around a divine oracle (vv2-5) interrupting the praise. "We give thanks to you, O God; we give thanks, for your name is near; we declare your wondrous deeds." The "nearness" of the divine name (qārôb šĕmekā) — YHWH's active presence rather than remote abstraction — is the ground of thanksgiving. John 1:14 presents Christ as the ultimate nearness of the divine name: the Word who "dwelt (eskēnōsen, tabernacled) among us." In Christ, the divine name is not merely near but personally embodied.
2: A direct connection: "When I choose the appointed time (mōʿēd), I will judge with perfect equity." This is the voice of YHWH speaking directly — the divine first-person oracle within the communal Psalm. The mōʿēd (appointed time, the same term used for the sacred calendar of Leviticus 23) is the eschatological judgment seat. Acts 17:31: "For he has set a day when he will judge the world with justice by the man he has appointed. He has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead." Christ is both the appointed time's judge and the proof of the appointment: the resurrection establishes who judges at the mōʿēd.
3: A revelation of God: "When the earth totters and all its people with it, it is I who hold its pillars firm." The divine upholding of creation — the pillars of the earth held firm when everything else totters — is the sovereignty behind all apparent chaos. Hebrews 1:3: "The Son is the radiance of God's glory and the exact representation of his being, sustaining all things by his powerful word." The divine upholding of the earth's pillars in Psalms 75:3 is the cosmic sustaining that Hebrews attributes to Christ — the one through whom and for whom all things were made (Colossians 1:16-17) holds everything together even when it appears to totter.
4: A revelation of God: "I say to the arrogant, 'Stop your boasting,' and to the wicked, 'Do not raise your horn.'" The divine rebuke of arrogance — the authoritative word that deflates boastful human power — is the pattern of Christ's confrontation with those who rely on worldly status. 1 Corinthians 1:27-29: "God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise; God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong... so that no one may boast before him." The divine anti-boasting of Psalms 75:4 reaches its climax in the cross, which shatters every human confidence except confidence in the crucified Messiah.
5: A revelation of God: "Do not raise your horn on high; do not speak with an arrogant neck." The "arrogant neck" (tĕdabbĕrû bĕṣawwār ʿātāq — speak with a stiff/insolent neck) is the idiom for the pride that refuses submission. James 4:6: "God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble." 1 Peter 5:5-6 applies this directly to the Christian community under Christ: "God opposes the proud but shows favor to the humble. Humble yourselves, therefore, under God's mighty hand, that he may lift you up in due time." The divine anti-arrogance oracle of Psalms 75:5 is the wisdom that Christ embodies (Matthew 11:29: "I am gentle and humble in heart") and instills in his community.
6: A revelation of God: "For advancement comes neither from east nor west, nor from the wilderness of the south." The negation of all human geographic sources of power — east, west, and south (the three compass points where human kingdoms originated: Babylon, Egypt, and the southern wilderness peoples) — is the Psalm's anti-human-politics declaration. Acts 4:12: "Salvation is found in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to mankind by which we must be saved." The "not from east, west, or south" of Psalms 75:6 becomes the "no other name" of Acts 4 — promotion/salvation comes from YHWH alone, enacted through Christ alone.
7: A revelation of God: "but God is the judge; he brings one person down and raises another up." The divine sovereignty over status — YHWH as the one who deposes and elevates — is the pattern of Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:52: "He has brought down rulers from their thrones but has lifted up the humble") and of the resurrection itself. The ultimate enactment of Psalms 75:7 is Christ: brought down to death (the ultimate humiliation) and raised up by the Father (the ultimate elevation). Acts 2:33: "Exalted to the right hand of God" — YHWH raised the one who was brought down, reversing the human verdict.
8: A direct connection: "For in the hand of the LORD there is a cup of foaming wine, well mixed; he pours it out, and all the wicked of the earth shall drain it to the very dregs." The cup of divine wrath — the cup that the wicked drain to the dregs — is the cup that Christ prays to have removed: Mark 14:36, "Abba, Father, everything is possible for you. Take this cup from me. Yet not what I will, but what you will." John 18:11: "Shall I not drink the cup the Father has given me?" Christ drinks the cup of Psalms 75:8 on behalf of those who deserve it — the wrath that should fall on the wicked falls on the one who takes their place. Revelation 14:10 and 16:19 show this cup finally poured out on those who reject the substitute who drank it.
9: A revelation of God: "But as for me, I will declare this forever; I will sing praises to the God of Jacob." The personal declaration of eternal praise amid the oracle of judgment is the doxological response to divine sovereignty. Hebrews 2:12 shows Christ himself as the leader of this eternal praise: "I will declare your name to my brothers and sisters; in the assembly I will sing your praises" (citing Psalms 22:22). The "forever" of Psalms 75:9's praise is the unceasing worship that Christ leads in the community of the resurrection — the great assembly where the risen one leads the praise of the God who raised him.
10: A direct connection: "All the horns of the wicked I will cut down, but the horns of the righteous shall be lifted high." The horn imagery — power symbolized as the horn of an ox (Deuteronomy 33:17; 1 Samuel 2:1) — reaches its Christological fulfillment in Zechariah's song at the birth of John the Baptist: Luke 1:69, "He has raised up a horn of salvation (keras sōtērias) for us in the house of his servant David." The divine promise of Psalms 75:10 — wicked horns cut, righteous horns lifted — is enacted in the resurrection: the horn of the crucified one (whose power was apparently broken) is raised as the definitive horn of salvation.
